headline: Venture Global, Cheniere Energy Stock Lifted by Natural-Gas Crunch
summary: Shares in Venture Global rose more than 11% in premarket trading as investors bet the U.S. exporter of liquefied-natural gas could benefit from Middle East supply disruption.  Shares in larger rival Cheniere Energy, also an LNG exporter, gained nearly 3% premarket, having advanced around 13% since the start of the war.  The conflict has severely impacted Middle Eastern countries' ability to produce and export natural gas.

headline: How The Exxon Mobil (XOM) Story Is Shifting With Oil Assumptions And Valuation Debates
summary: The Street fair value price target for Exxon Mobil has shifted from about US$144.25 to roughly US$151.42, giving you an updated anchor for how analysts are framing the stock today. Behind that move, research is weighing higher and lower oil price assumptions, Middle East supply risks, and the extent to which these factors might shape cash generation and capital returns. As you read on, you will see how to interpret these changing targets and keep up with the evolving narrative around Exxon...

headline: Exxon, Shell Now in the Line of Fire in Iran War
summary: Western oil companies are now in the direct line of fire in the Iran War.  Separate attacks by Iran over the past 24 hours damaged facilities owned by  Shell  and part-owned by Exxon Mobil.  Exxon stock was up 1.2% on Thursday, while Shell fell 0.6%.

headline: Gas Facility Damage Will Reduce Qatar’s Export Capacity by 17%
summary: Iranian strikes on liquefied natural gas facilities at Qatar’s Ras Laffan Industrial City reduced the country’s export capacity by 17% and will take three to five years to repair, the country’s energy minister said.  The attacks damaged two liquefied natural gas producing trains that are both joint ventures with ExxonMobil and will cost about $20 billion a year in lost revenue, said Saad Sherida Al-Kaabi, who is also chief executive of QatarEnergy.  The outage affects the production of several associated products, including a 14% reduction in Qatar’s exports of helium, which is critical to the production of semiconductors.

content.title: Gas Facility Damage Will Reduce Qatar’s Export Capacity by 17%
content.summary: Iranian strikes on liquefied natural gas facilities at Qatar’s Ras Laffan Industrial City reduced the country’s export capacity by 17% and will take three to five years to repair, the country’s energy minister said.  The attacks damaged two liquefied natural gas producing trains that are both joint ventures with ExxonMobil and will cost about $20 billion a year in lost revenue, said Saad Sherida Al-Kaabi, who is also chief executive of QatarEnergy.  The outage affects the production of several associated products, including a 14% reduction in Qatar’s exports of helium, which is critical to the production of semiconductors.
